subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that if x 2 – 5x + 6 = 0, then x = 3 or x = 2
question_html: Show that if x2 – 5x + 6 = 0, then x = 3 or x = 2
solution_text: {tex}x^{2}-5 x+6=0 \text { (given) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x – 3) (x – 2) = 0 (replacing an expression by an equal/equivalent expression) ⇒ x – 3 = 0 or x – 2 = 0 (from the established theorem ab = 0 ⇒ either a = 0 or b = 0, for a, b in R) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x – 3 + 3 = 0 + 3 or x – 2 + 2 = 0 + 2 (adding equal quantities on either side of the equation does not alter the nature of the equation) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 0 = 3 or x + 0 = 2 (using the identity property of integers under addition) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 or x = 2 (using the identity property of integers under addition) Hence, x 2 – 5x + 6 = 0 implies x = 3 or x = 2
solution_html:
{tex}x^{2}-5 x+6=0 \text { (given) }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x – 3) (x – 2) = 0 (replacing an expression by an equal/equivalent expression) ⇒ x – 3 = 0 or x – 2 = 0 (from the established theorem ab = 0 ⇒ either a = 0 or b = 0, for a, b in R)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x – 3 + 3 = 0 + 3 or x – 2 + 2 = 0 + 2 (adding equal quantities on either side of the equation does not alter the nature of the equation)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 0 = 3 or x + 0 = 2 (using the identity property of integers under addition)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 or x = 2 (using the identity property of integers under addition)
Hence, x2 – 5x + 6 = 0 implies x = 3 or x = 2

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function f : R → R defined by f (x) = 2x + 5 is one-one.
question_html: Prove that the function f : R → R defined by f (x) = 2x + 5 is one-one.
solution_text: Note that a function f is one-one if f(x 1 ) = f (x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 (definition of one-one function) Now, given that f (x 1 ) = f (x 2 ), i.e., 2x 1 + 5 = 2x 2 + 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 1 + 5 – 5 = 2x 2 + 5 – 5 (adding the same quantity on both sides) 2x 1 + 0 = 2x 2 + 0 2x 1 = 2x 2 ( using additive identity of real number) {tex}\frac{2}{2} x_{1}=\frac{2}{2} x_{2}{/tex} dividing by the same non zero quantity x 1 = x 2 Hence, the given function is one one
solution_html:
Note that a function f is one-one if
f(x1 ) = f (x2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2 (definition of one-one function)
Now, given that f (x1 ) = f (x2 ), i.e., 2x1 + 5 = 2x2 + 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x1 + 5 – 5 = 2x2 + 5 – 5 (adding the same quantity on both sides)
2x1 + 0 = 2x2 + 0
2x1 = 2x2 ( using additive identity of real number)
{tex}\frac{2}{2} x_{1}=\frac{2}{2} x_{2}{/tex} dividing by the same non zero quantity
x1 = x2
Hence, the given function is one one

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that if {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right], \text { then } A^{n}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos n \theta & \sin n \theta \\ -\sin n \theta & \cos n \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Show that if {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right], \text { then } A^{n}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos n \theta & \sin n \theta \\ -\sin n \theta & \cos n \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: we have {tex}P(n): A^{n}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos n \theta & \sin n \theta \\ -\sin n \theta & \cos n \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} we note that {tex}\mathrm{P}(1): \mathrm{A}^{1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} Therefore, p (1) is true. Assume that p(k) is true i,e. {tex}P(k): A^{*}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos k \theta & \sin k \theta \\ -\sin k \theta & \cos k \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} We want to prove that P (k +1) is true whenever p(k) is true ie {tex}P(k+1): A^{k+1}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos (k+1) \theta & \sin (k+1) \theta \\ -\sin (k+1) \theta & \cos (k+1) \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now {tex}A^{i+1}=A^{k} \cdot A{/tex} Since p(k) is true, we have {tex}A^{\Delta 1}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} \cos k \theta & \sin k \theta \\ -\sin k \theta & \cos k \theta \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos k \theta \cos \theta-\sin k \theta \sin \theta & \cos k \theta \sin \theta+\sin k \theta \cos \theta \\ -\sin k \theta \cos \theta-\cos k \theta \sin \theta & -\sin k \theta \sin \theta+\cos k \theta \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos (k+1) \theta & \sin (k+1) \theta \\ -\sin (k+1) \theta & \cos (k+1) \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex} Thus, P(k + 1) is true whenever P(k) is true. Hence, P(n) is true for all n ≥ 1 (by the principle of mathematical induction).
solution_html:
we have
{tex}P(n): A^{n}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos n \theta & \sin n \theta \\ -\sin n \theta & \cos n \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
we note that {tex}\mathrm{P}(1): \mathrm{A}^{1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Therefore, p (1) is true.
Assume that p(k) is true i,e.
{tex}P(k): A^{*}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos k \theta & \sin k \theta \\ -\sin k \theta & \cos k \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We want to prove that P (k +1) is true whenever p(k) is true ie
{tex}P(k+1): A^{k+1}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos (k+1) \theta & \sin (k+1) \theta \\ -\sin (k+1) \theta & \cos (k+1) \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now        {tex}A^{i+1}=A^{k} \cdot A{/tex}
Since  p(k) is true, we have
{tex}A^{\Delta 1}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} \cos k \theta & \sin k \theta \\ -\sin k \theta & \cos k \theta \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos k \theta \cos \theta-\sin k \theta \sin \theta & \cos k \theta \sin \theta+\sin k \theta \cos \theta \\ -\sin k \theta \cos \theta-\cos k \theta \sin \theta & -\sin k \theta \sin \theta+\cos k \theta \cos \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \cos (k+1) \theta & \sin (k+1) \theta \\ -\sin (k+1) \theta & \cos (k+1) \theta \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Thus, P(k + 1) is true whenever P(k) is true.
Hence, P(n) is true for all n ≥ 1 (by the principle of mathematical induction).

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that in any triangle ABC a = b cos C + c cos B
question_html: Show that in any triangle ABC a = b cos C + c cos B
solution_text: Let p be the statement “ABC is any triangle” and q be the statement “ a = b cos C + c cos B” Let ABC be a triangle. From A draw AD perpendicular to BC (BC produced if necessary). As we know that any triangle has to be either acute or obtuse or right-angled, we can split p into three statements r, s and t, where r : ABC is an acute-angled triangle with {tex}\angle{/tex} C is acute. s : ABC is an obtuse-angled triangle with {tex}\angle{/tex} C is obtuse t : ABC is a right-angled triangle with ∠C is a right angle. Hence, we prove the theorem by three cases case (i) When C is acute (Fig. From the right-angled triangle ADB, {tex}\frac{B D}{A B}=\cos E{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{AB} \cos \mathrm{B}{/tex} {tex}=c \cos B{/tex} from the right-angled triangle ADC {tex}\frac{C D}{\Delta C}=\cos C{/tex} CD = AC cos C = b cos C a = BD + CD = c cos B + b cos C ...(1) case(ii) When ∠ C is obtuse in below figure. From the right angled triangle ADB, {tex}BD \over AB{/tex} = cos B i.e. BD = ABcos B = c cos B From the right angled triangle ADC, {tex}\frac{C D}{A C}=\cos \angle A C D{/tex} = cos (180 o - C) = - cos C i.e. CD = - AC cos C = - b cos C Now a = BC = BD - CD i.e. a = c cos B - ( - b cos C) a = c cos B + b cos C ... (2) Case (iii) When {tex}\angle C{/tex} is a right angle in the given figure. From the right angled triangle ACB, {tex}BC \over AB{/tex} = cosB i.e. BC = AB cos B a = c cos B, and b cos C = b cos 900 = 0. Thus, we may write a = 0 + c cos B = b cos C + c cos B ... (3) From (1), (2) and (3). We assert that for any triangle ABC, a = b cos C + c cos B By case (i), r {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved. By case (ii), s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved. By case (iii), t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved. Hence, from the proof by cases, (r v s v t) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved, i.e., p {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved.
solution_html:
Let p be the statement “ABC is any triangle” and q be the statement “
a = b cos C + c cos B”
Let ABC be a triangle. From A draw AD perpendicular to BC (BC produced if necessary).
As we know that any triangle has to be either acute or obtuse or right-angled, we can split p into three statements r, s and t, where
r : ABC is an acute-angled triangle with {tex}\angle{/tex}C is acute.
s : ABC is an obtuse-angled triangle with {tex}\angle{/tex}C is obtuse
t : ABC is a right-angled triangle with ∠C is a right angle.
Hence, we prove the theorem by three cases
case (i) When C is acute (Fig.
From the right-angled triangle ADB,
{tex}\frac{B D}{A B}=\cos E{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{AB} \cos \mathrm{B}{/tex}
{tex}=c \cos B{/tex}

from the right-angled triangle ADC                            
{tex}\frac{C D}{\Delta C}=\cos C{/tex}
CD = AC cos C
= b cos C
a = BD + CD
= c cos B + b cos C ...(1)
case(ii) When ∠ C is obtuse in below figure.

From the right angled triangle ADB,
{tex}BD \over AB{/tex} = cos B
i.e. BD = ABcos B
= c cos B From the right angled triangle ADC,
{tex}\frac{C D}{A C}=\cos \angle A C D{/tex}
= cos (180o - C) = - cos C i.e. CD = - AC cos C = - b cos C
Now a = BC = BD - CD
i.e. a = c cos B - ( - b cos C)
a = c cos B + b cos C ... (2)
Case (iii) When {tex}\angle C{/tex} is a right angle in the given figure.

From the right angled triangle ACB,
{tex}BC \over AB{/tex} = cosB
i.e. BC = AB cos B
a = c cos B,
and b cos C = b cos 900 = 0.
Thus, we may write a = 0 + c cos B
= b cos C + c cos B ... (3)
From (1), (2) and (3). We assert that for any triangle ABC,
a = b cos C + c cos B
By case (i), r {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved.
By case (ii), s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved.
By case (iii), t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved.
Hence, from the proof by cases, (r v s v t) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved, i.e., p {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q is proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the set of all prime numbers is infinite.
question_html: Show that the set of all prime numbers is infinite.
solution_text: Let P be the set of all prime numbers. We take the negation of the statement “the set of all prime numbers is infinite”, i.e., we assume the set of all prime numbers to be finite. Hence, we can list all the prime numbers as P 1 , P 2 , P 3 ,..., P k (say). Note that we have assumed that there is no prime number other than P 1 , P 2 , P 3 ,..., P k . Now consider N = (P 1 , P 2 , P 3 ,..., P k + 1) ... (i) N is not in the list as N is larger than any of the numbers in the list. N is either prime or composite. If N is a prime, then by (i), there exists a prime number which is not listed. On the other hand, if N is composite, it should have a prime divisor. But none of the numbers in the list can divide N, because they all leave the remainder 1. Hence, the prime divisor should be other than the one in the list. Thus, in both the cases whether N is a prime or a composite, we ended up with contradiction to the fact that we have listed all the prime numbers. Hence, our assumption that a set of all prime numbers is finite is false. Thus, the set of all prime numbers is infinite.
solution_html:
Let P be the set of all prime numbers. We take the negation of the statement “the set of all prime numbers is infinite”, i.e., we assume the set of all prime numbers to be finite. Hence, we can list all the prime numbers as P1 , P2 , P3 ,..., Pk (say). Note that we have assumed that there is no prime number other than P1 , P2 , P3 ,..., Pk . Now consider N = (P1 , P2 , P3 ,..., Pk  + 1) ... (i)
N is not in the list as N is larger than any of the numbers in the list. N is either prime or composite. If N is a prime, then by (i), there exists a prime number which is not listed. On the other hand, if N is composite, it should have a prime divisor. But none of the numbers in the list can divide N, because they all leave the remainder 1. Hence, the prime divisor should be other than the one in the list. Thus, in both the cases whether N is a prime or a composite, we ended up with contradiction to the fact that we have listed all the prime numbers. Hence, our assumption that a set of all prime numbers is finite is false. Thus, the set of all prime numbers is infinite.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function f : R → R defined by f (x) = 2x + 5 is one-one.
question_html: Prove that the function f : R → R defined by f (x) = 2x + 5 is one-one.
solution_text: Note that a function f is one-one if f(x 1 ) = f (x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 (definition of one-one function) Now, given that f (x 1 ) = f (x 2 ), i.e., 2x 1 + 5 = 2x 2 + 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 1 + 5 – 5 = 2x 2 + 5 – 5 (adding the same quantity on both sides) 2x 1 + 0 = 2x 2 + 0 2x 1 = 2x 2 ( using additive identity of real number) {tex}\frac{2}{2} x_{1}=\frac{2}{2} x_{2}{/tex} dividing by the same non zero quantity x 1 = x 2 Hence, the given function is one one
solution_html:
Note that a function f is one-one if
f(x1 ) = f (x2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2 (definition of one-one function)
Now, given that f (x1 ) = f (x2 ), i.e., 2x1 + 5 = 2x2 + 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x1 + 5 – 5 = 2x2 + 5 – 5 (adding the same quantity on both sides)
2x1 + 0 = 2x2 + 0
2x1 = 2x2 ( using additive identity of real number)
{tex}\frac{2}{2} x_{1}=\frac{2}{2} x_{2}{/tex} dividing by the same non zero quantity
x1 = x2
Hence, the given function is one one

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that if a matrix A is invertible, then A is non-singular.
question_html: Show that if a matrix A is invertible, then A is non-singular.
solution_text: Writing the above statement in symbolic form, we have p ⇒ q, where, p is “matrix A is invertible” and q is “A is non singular” Instead of proving the given statement, we prove its contrapositive statement, i.e., if A is not a non singular matrix, then the matrix A is not invertible. If A is not a non singular matrix, then it means the matrix A is singular, i.e., |A| = 0 Then {tex}\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{a d j \mathrm{A}}{| \mathrm{Al}} \text { does not exist as } \mathrm{|A|}=0{/tex} Hence, A is not invertible. Thus, we have proved that if A is not a non singular matrix, then A is not invertible. i.e -q = -p Hence, if a matrix A is invertible, then A is non singular.
solution_html:
Writing the above statement in symbolic form, we have p ⇒ q, where, p is “matrix A is invertible” and q is “A is non singular”
Instead of proving the given statement, we prove its contrapositive statement, i.e., if A is not a non singular matrix, then the matrix A is not invertible.
If A is not a non singular matrix, then it means the matrix A is singular, i.e.,
|A| = 0
Then {tex}\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{a d j \mathrm{A}}{| \mathrm{Al}} \text { does not exist as } \mathrm{|A|}=0{/tex}
Hence, A is not invertible.
Thus, we have proved that if A is not a non singular matrix, then A is not invertible.
i.e -q = -p
Hence, if a matrix A is invertible, then A is non singular.
